robotic-assisted supravesicular hernia repair with mesh by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is an eighty years old male who presented with a right groin bulge with unenough pain and discomfort. Six months prior to this visit, he underwent a right inguinal hernia repair with patch and plug technique by another surgeon. However, he continued to have the same bulge in his right groin since the day of initial surgery. Therefore, he underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy and found to have a right supravesical hernia, which was not recognized during his initial operation.

Therefore, he underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy and found to have a right supravesical hernia, which was not recognized during his initial operation. This video demonstrates the robotic-assisted tap supravesical hernia repair with mesh. Robotic approach allowed the surgeon to diagnose and repair this unrecognized rare supravesical hernia.

The robotic-assisted procedure reveals a supravesical hernia as a visible defect in the supravesical fossa. The fossa is a triangular area bounded laterally and superiorly by the median and medial umbilical ligaments, and inferiorly by the peritoneal reflection extending from the anterior abdominal wall to the dome of the bladder.

The supravesical hernia is visible as a defect in the supravesical fossa. The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, entering the preperitoneal space and creating a preperitoneal flap. Robotic instruments are used to precisely dissect the peritoneal flap from the mesh, revealing scarring. The dissection continues medially to expose the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The preperitoneal space is entered, and a preperitoneal flap is created. Robotic instruments are used to precisely dissect the peritoneal flap from the mesh, revealing scarring from a previous mesh plug. The dissection continues medially to expose the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon continues dissection medially towards the supravesical hernia, reducing the perivesical fat that was incarcerated into the hernia defect. The supravesical hernia defect is clearly visualized, along with the exposed mesh plug and bus difference. The hernia sac has been reduced.

The surgeon continues medial dissection towards the supravesical hernia, reducing the perivesical fat incarcerated in the hernia defect. The supravesical hernia defect is clearly visualized, along with the mesh plug and bus difference. The hernia sac is reduced, and a three-dimensional mesh from BARD is inserted into the preperitoneal pocket to cover all defects, including the supravesical perineal defect.

A three-dimensional mesh from BARD is inserted into the preperitoneal pocket, covering the supravesical perineal defect. The mesh is secured to Cooper's ligaments using etabond in a simple, interrupted fashion. Fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh to adhere it to the abdominal wall. The peritoneal defect is repaired with a two-hole PDS V-lock suture in a simple running fashion.

The surgeon secures the mesh to Cooper's ligaments using Etibond sutures in a simple, interrupted fashion. Fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh within the peritoneal pocket to adhere it to the abdominal wall. The peritoneal defect is then repaired using a two-hole PDS V-lock suture in a simple running fashion.
Supravascular hernia is a rare hernia and difficult to diagnose. Robotic platform allowed the surgeon to diagnose and repair this rare hernia with minimally invasive approach.
